title: You don't need to call it "vibe coding" - but Cursor and friends can help you amplify your impact
author: Iago Martinez Iago Martinez Published Mar
text:
You don't need to call it "vibe coding" - but Cursor and friends can help you amplify your impact
I jokingly refer to myself as "post-technical" - I love coding, but 90% of my job is leadership and communication. The rise and rise of AI-enabled development tools made me curious: how can I reconnect with making an impact through code?
The TL;DR
I did a task that normally would have taken me a week, in 4 hours.
How? A combination of Copilot and Cursor - and I kept screenshots along the way to write it up after.
This is not scientific, think of it more as a "vibe review" than a rigorous, double-blind study.
I used both Copilot and Cursor with "out of the box settings":
First attempt: Github Copilot
I wanted to change some python code I wrote to extract data for our annual process to claim RnD tax relief - find out more here.
The process needed some new logic to deal with overlapping effort between tasks. For context, last year this took hours of manual work - by multiple people - to troubleshoot. Nothing overly complex - but given I don't have the space for focused, uninterrupted coding, this would have taken me days.
First up I tried Github copilot:
Copilot: Conclusions
Enter Cursor
At this point I switched to Cursor, and changed my approach from micro-managing the tool by taking small steps, to starting out by brainstorming:
The source data for <my code file> is from our work tracking tool. It lists the start and end date of work done by developers.There are records where the dates overlap. This is a data problem which affects accuracy, because people will appear as working on more than one thing at the same time.
I want to work out how to fix this, please help me brainstorm an algorithm to deal with duplicates as follows:
Row A:
Owner: Charlie, Id: 1, Start date: 2024-01-08, End date: 2024-01-11, Working days: 3
Row B:Owner: Charlie, Id: 2, Start date: 2024-01-09, End date: 2024-01-12, Working days: 3
In this case, there is an overlap of 3 days. To resolve this, I want to work out an adjustment which reduces days, such that the overlap is distributed across both rows.
This is the simplest scenario, but let's start here.
As I went through this, I narrowed my own understanding of the problem:
Recommended by LinkedIn
When I was at a good point, I went ahead and asked Cursor to implement the code for me:
Finally, I extended the code to:
Cursor: Conclusions
The code that was generated did the job, but it was rough; there were elements of duplication (simplest possible code broke the DRY principle) - but the real value was in the process of narrowing down a solution, using Cursor as a second brain that can churn out working code quicker than I can.
Cursor can "run away" with changes and go beyond what has been asked - the remedy for this is to keep the prompts tight.
Other lessons:
The Verdict: Cursor wins (for my use case)
Breaking down Copilot and Cursor with a more structured lens - not as a scientific study, but as a practical "vibe assessment" for fellow engineers:
Code Generation Speed
Copilot: ★★★☆☆ (Slower, requires manual application)
Cursor: ★★★★★ (Seamless, quick iterations)
Context Understanding
Copilot: ★★★☆☆ (Struggles with complex, multi-file prompts)
Cursor: ★★★★☆ (Better at zooming out, understanding broader context)
Prompt Handling
Copilot: ★★★☆☆ (Tends to break down with broad/unclear prompts)
Cursor: ★★★★☆ (More adaptable, handles vague requirements better)
Code Quality
Copilot: ★★☆☆☆ (Occasional nonsensical suggestions)
Cursor: ★★★☆☆ (More coherent, though still requires review)
My hot take? Cursor currently has the edge - it trumps Copilot on speed, and "intelligence"- but this landscape is changing faster than we can write code.
I received anecdotal feedback from several engineers in our team that Copilot's code prediction functionality is superior to Cursor, and so they tend to use that more for hands-on, all-day coding - so YMMV - the most important thing is to try out multiple tools and work out what's best for your use case.
Go for impact
I would have dragged my feet for weeks in the past, looking for the right time to sit down and code. Instead - Cursor helped me achieve a week's worth of work in 4 hours - and this will save a lot of time of post-hoc analysis, for multiple people. That's a valuable, quantifiable impact.
AI coding assistants like Copilot and Cursor aren't just productivity enhancers; they're potential catalysts for reimagining how we work.
This introduces new challenges: ensuring code quality, evals, maintaining architectural integrity, new security vulnerabilities - and preventing over-reliance on AI-generated solutions. Understanding how these models work under the hood, and taking an empirical approach to working with them will change our relationship from "magic code writer" to "a tool that can multiply my impact and speed through considered prompting" - see this article by Birgitta Böckeler for a discussion of why it's not a silver bullet, with some practical thoughts for how to make this work.
To paraphrase Adam Martin's recent CTO Craft talk - "go for 20X impact, don't bother with the small 2X impact tasks". Try this out for yourself, and let me know - how have you applied these kind of tools to help you be more impactful? What tools are working for you?
